headline: A Humble Plant Could Transform Malaria Prevention in Uganda
meta_description: Welsh and Ugandan researchers discover that homegrown catnip is as effective as DEET for mosquito repellent—and far more affordable.
summary_short: A collaborative team found that catnip lotion works as well as DEET to repel mosquitoes, offering rural Uganda a low-cost alternative to expensive medications.
body:
Subsistence farmers in rural Uganda face a pressing problem: malaria medications are becoming less reliable as the parasite develops drug resistance. Now, researchers believe they've found an unlikely solution growing in gardens across the region.

At a science conference in Florence, Italy, Welsh and Ugandan researchers presented findings showing that catnip plants produce a chemical compound called nepetalactone that repels mosquitoes just as effectively as DEET. In laboratory tests, a 6% nepetalactone skin lotion matched DEET's performance, while a 2% solution proved only slightly less effective.

"DEET is out of the price bracket for most rural Ugandan subsistence farmers," explains Dr. Simon Scofield, a senior lecturer at Cardiff University. The team wanted to create something both highly effective and affordable—something locals could produce themselves.

Catnip is remarkably practical. The plant grows easily, its essential oil distills simply, and it smells far better than DEET. Remarkably, people have relied on it as a mosquito repellent for centuries.

The resulting lotion cannot be patented because it uses only essential oil with stabilizers and cosmetic bases. This means no single company controls production. Instead, Scofield envisions a self-sustaining system where money flows back to farmers and producers at every stage, transforming prevention from grant-dependent work into a living economy.
